subject: Physics
q: In photoelectric experiment energy of $2.48 \mathrm{eV}$ irradiates a photo sensitive material. The stopping potential was measured to be $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$. Work function of the photo sensitive material is :
question: In photoelectric experiment energy of $2.48 \mathrm{eV}$ irradiates a photo sensitive material. The stopping potential was measured to be $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$. Work function of the photo sensitive material is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{h} \nu-\phi \\ & 0.5 \mathrm{~V}=2.48-\phi \\ & \text { work function }(\phi)=2.48 \mathrm{~V}-0.5 \mathrm{~V}=1.98 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

q:
The magnetic field associated with a light wave is given, at the origin, by B=B0 sin⁡3.14×107ct+sin⁡6.28×107ct. If this light falls on a silver plate having a work function of 4.7eV, what will be the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons?

(c=3×108 m s−1,h=6.6×10−34 J s)
question:

(c=3×108 m s−1,h=6.6×10−34 J s)
solution:
B=B0sin⁡3.14×107ct+sin⁡6.28×107ct

Maximum KE will associate with the maximum frequency

fmax⁡ =6.28×107c2π=107×3×108 Hz           =3×1015 Hz

KEmax=hfmax-ϕ

=6.63×10-34×3×10151.6×10-19-4.7 eV

=7.72 eV

question: Sodium and copper have work functions $2.3 \mathrm{eV}$ and $4.5 \mathrm{eV}$ respectively. Then the ratio of the wave lengths is nearest to

q: Two sources of light emit with a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The ratio of number of photons of visible light emitted by each source having wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> respectively, will be :
question: Two sources of light emit with a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The ratio of number of photons of visible light emitted by each source having wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> respectively, will be :
solution:
Let n1 and n2 be the number of photons coming out from the first and the second source of light respectively per second.

So, for the first source,

n1×hcλ1=200   ...1

And, for the second source,

n2×hcλ2=200   ...2

From equations (1) and (2), it implies that

n1n2=λ1λ2=300500⇒n1n2=35

q: The ratio of wavelengths of proton and deuteron accelerated by potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. Then, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> will be
question: The ratio of wavelengths of proton and deuteron accelerated by potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. Then, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> will be
solution:
Kinetic energy gained by a charged particle accelerated by a potential V is qV

KE=qV

Using relation between momentum and kinetic energy

⇒p22 m=qV⇒p=2mqV

We know that momentum of charged particle is p=hλ.

Thus, λ=h2mqV

Now, λpλd=mdVdmpVp

⇒12=2VdVp⇒VpVd=4

subject: Physics
q:
In a photoelectric experiment, ultraviolet light of wavelength 280 nm is used with lithium cathode having work function ϕ=2.5 eV. If the wavelength of incident light is switched to 400 nm, find out the change in the stopping potential.

h=6.63×10-34 J s, c=3×108 m s-1
question:
In a photoelectric experiment, ultraviolet light of wavelength 280 nm is used with lithium cathode having work function ϕ=2.5 eV. If the wavelength of incident light is switched to 400 nm, find out the change in the stopping potential.

h=6.63×10-34 J s, c=3×108 m s-1
solution:
From the photoelectric equation,

eV0=hcλ−ϕ

Using this equation for the given two cases,

eV1=hc280 nm−ϕ

eV2=hc400 nm−ϕ

On subtracting,

eV1−V2=1240120280×400                   =1.3 eV

⇒V1−V2=1.3 V

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an α-particle are accelerated from rest by 2 V and 4 V potentials, respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is :
question: A proton and an α-particle are accelerated from rest by 2 V and 4 V potentials, respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is :
solution:
The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the proton is given by

λp=h2mpqpV   ...1

The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the alpha particle is given by

λα=h2mαqαV'   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

λpλα=mαmp×qαV'qpV   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

λpλα=41×2×41×2=4:1

If the same surface is illuminated by radiation of double the previous wavelength, then the stopping potential becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The threshold wavelength of the metal is:
question: When radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is incident on a metallic surface, the stopping potential of ejected photoelectrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The threshold wavelength of the metal is:
solution:
VS=hv-ϕ

4.8=hcλ-ϕ  ...(i)

1.6=hc2λ-ϕ ...(ii)

Using above equation (i) -(ii)

3.2=hcλ-hc2λ

3.2=hc2λ   ...(iii) 

λ=hc6.4

Put in equation (ii)

ϕ=1.6

hcλth=1.6

λth=hc1.6

=hc6.4×4=4λ

subject: Physics
q: Light is incident on a metallic plate having work function $110 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}$. If the produced photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy then the angular frequency of the incident light is $\_\_\_\_$ rad/s. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}\right)$.
question: Light is incident on a metallic plate having work function $110 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}$. If the produced photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy then the angular frequency of the incident light is $\_\_\_\_$ rad/s. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}\right)$.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $h u = W + KE$.
With zero kinetic energy, $h u = W$.
The frequency is $ u = rac{W}{h} = rac{110 imes 10^{-20}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}} = 16.59 imes 10^{14}$ Hz.
The angular frequency is $\omega = 2\pi u = 2\pi imes 16.59 imes 10^{14}$
$= 104.25 imes 10^{14} = 1.04 imes 10^{16}$ rad/s.

q: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
question: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
options: Half of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Four times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
<math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></math>  of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Two times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength is given as

λ=hp

∵p22m=k⇒p=2mk

⇒λ=h2mk

At ground level energy =E0 so

At n=4 level energy =E0n2=E016

∴ at ground level

λ0=h2m E0

At n = 4 level

λ′=h2mE016=4h2m E0

⇒λ′=4λ0

subject: Physics
q: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
question: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
options: All the metals will give out photo electrons with same kinetic energies.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by:

$K_{ ext{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident radiation and $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal.

The work function is related to the threshold frequency $ u_0$ by $\Phi = h u_0$. From the given graph, the x-intercept represents the threshold frequency. We can observe that:

$ u_0(X_1) < u_0(X_2) < u_0(X_3)$

Therefore, the work functions of the metals follow the order:

$\Phi(X_1) < \Phi(X_2) < \Phi(X_3)$

For the same wavelength $\lambda$ of incident radiation, the term $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ is constant. Thus, the kinetic energy $K_{ ext{max}}$ will be greatest for the metal with the smallest work function.

Since metal $X_1$ has the minimum work function, it will emit electrons with the greatest kinetic energy.

Answer: $X_1$

λ=hp=h2mE∝1m

mα>mp>me

so λe>λp>λα

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math> How much extra energy must be given to this particle so that the de-Broglie wavelength reduces to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the initial value?
solution:
Recall the formula of de-Broglie wavelength, λ=hp, here p is the linear momentum of the particle, now use the relation between linear momentum and kinetic energy, p=2Em⇒E∝1λ2⇒E=kλ2

and E'=16k9λ2⇒ΔE=7E9.

question:

Statement 2 : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.
options: Statement $1$ is false, Statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is false
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is the correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is true, Statement $2$ is not the correct explanation for statement $1$.
solution: Davisson - Germer experiment showed that electron beams can undergo diffraction when passed through atomic crystals. This shows the wave nature of electrons as waves can exhibit interference and diffraction.

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength for an electron accelerated through the potential difference of $V_1$ volt is $\lambda_1$. When the potential difference is changed to $V_2$ volt, the associated de Broglie wavelength is increased by $50\%$. If $(V_1/V_2) = (9/\alpha)$, then the value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: The de Broglie wavelength for an electron accelerated through the potential difference of $V_1$ volt is $\lambda_1$. When the potential difference is changed to $V_2$ volt, the associated de Broglie wavelength is increased by $50\%$. If $(V_1/V_2) = (9/\alpha)$, then the value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$.

This implies $\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{V}}$.

Given that the new wavelength $\lambda_2$ is increased by $50\%$, we have $\lambda_2 = \lambda_1 + 0.5\lambda_1 = \dfrac{3}{2}\lambda_1$.

Taking the ratio of the wavelengths, we get $\dfrac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}}$.

Substituting $\lambda_2 = \dfrac{3}{2}\lambda_1$, we get $\dfrac{2}{3} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}}$.

Squaring both sides yields $\dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{4}{9}$, which gives $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{9}{4}$.

Comparing this with the given relation $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{9}{\alpha}$, we find $\alpha = 4$.

Answer: $4$

q:
The equation λ=1.227x nm can be used to find the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron. In this equation x stands for :
Where,
m= mass of electron
P= momentum of electron
K= Kinetic energy of electron
V= Accelerating potential in volts for electron
question:
The equation λ=1.227x nm can be used to find the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron. In this equation x stands for :
Where,
m= mass of electron
P= momentum of electron
K= Kinetic energy of electron
V= Accelerating potential in volts for electron
solution:
The de-Broglie's wavelength is given by λ=hmv, where, h is Planck's constant and v is velocity.

Using relation between momentum and kinetic energy, we have λ=h2 mKE

Here, kinetic energy of electron is stated as KE=qV.

Then, we have λ=h2mqV

Putting the values of m=9.1×10-31 kg and q=1.62×10-19 C of electron

We get, λ=1.22V nm.

So, x stand for V.

question: An electron having de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is incident on a target in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-ray tube. λ=hmv

Kinetic energy, P22 m=h22 mλ2=hcλc

λc=2 mλ2ch

q: An electron, a doubly ionized helium ion ($He^{++}$) and proton are having the same kinetic energy. The relation between their respective de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>e</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>He</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></math> is :
question: An electron, a doubly ionized helium ion ($He^{++}$) and proton are having the same kinetic energy. The relation between their respective de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>e</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><msup><mi>He</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></math> is :
solution:
$\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m k}}$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}: \lambda_{\mathrm{p}}: \lambda_{{He}^{++}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{He}^{++}}}$
$\frac{1}{\sqrt{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-27}}}: \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}}=84: 2: 1$

question: The speed of electrons in a scanning electron microscope is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Resolving power RP∝1λ

λ=hP=hmv

So, RP∝mvh
RP∝P

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is $\lambda_e$ and the de Broglie wavelength associated with a proton accelerated through the same potential difference is $\lambda_p$. If their corresponding masses are $m_e$ and $m_p$, respectively, then the ratio of their de Broglie wavelengths $\left(\dfrac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p}\right)$ is ______.
question: The de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is $\lambda_e$ and the de Broglie wavelength associated with a proton accelerated through the same potential difference is $\lambda_p$. If their corresponding masses are $m_e$ and $m_p$, respectively, then the ratio of their de Broglie wavelengths $\left(\dfrac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p}\right)$ is ______.
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of a particle accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2mqV}}$.

For an electron, the charge is $e$ and mass is $m_e$. Its de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda_e = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV}}$.

For a proton, the charge is $e$ and mass is $m_p$. Its de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda_p = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_p eV}}$.

Taking the ratio of the two wavelengths, we get $\dfrac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p} = \dfrac{\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV}}}{\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_p eV}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m_p}{m_e}}$.

Answer: $\sqrt{\dfrac{m_p}{m_e}}$

subject: Physics
q: The radiation pressure exerted by a 450 W light source on a perfectly reflecting surface placed at 2 m away from it, is :
question: The radiation pressure exerted by a 450 W light source on a perfectly reflecting surface placed at 2 m away from it, is :
solution:
$P_{\mathrm{rad}}=\frac{2 I}{\mathrm{C}}$
Where $\mathrm{I}=$ intensity at surface
$\mathrm{C}=$ Speed of light
$\begin{aligned}
& I=\frac{\text { Power }}{\text { Area }}=\frac{450}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2} \\ & =\frac{450}{4 \pi \times 4}=\frac{450}{16 \pi} \\ & P_{\mathrm{rad}}=\frac{2 \times 450}{16 \pi \times 3 \times 10^8}=\frac{150}{8 \pi \times 10^8}
\end{aligned}$
$=5.97 \times 10^{-8} \approx 6 \times 10^{-8}$ Pascals
